nativeRange.habitat: Large river main channels and tributaries with rocky substrates and areas of submerged driftwood; prefers moderate to strong current with well-oxygenated water

decorPreferences: large driftwood pieces and bogwood for shelter and territory
rocky caves and overhangs as daytime retreats
smooth rounded substrate (sand or fine gravel)
dim areas with subdued lighting or floating plants to reduce light intensity
robust or artificial plants (S. decorus may uproot delicate plants)
secure-fitting lid — can be a strong jumper

diet.notes: Opportunistic omnivore strongly favouring meaty/protein-rich foods. Accepts high-quality sinking pellets, catfish wafers, frozen or live bloodworms, earthworms, prawns, and mussel. Supplement with algae wafers and blanched vegetables (courgette, cucumber) to provide plant matter. Feed primarily after lights-out in juvenile specimens; adults become more active during daytime and can be trained to feeding at consistent times. Avoid feeding exclusively on dried foods — dietary variety reduces susceptibility to vitamin deficiencies.

goodWith: large robust tetras (Congo tetras, bleeding heart tetras)
medium to large barbs (Denison barbs, tinfoil barbs)
other similarly-sized Synodontis (with sufficient space and caves)
large peaceful cichlids (Geophagus, severum) with caution
armoured catfish of similar size (large Ancistrus, Pterygoplichthys in large tanks)
avoidWith: small fish that fit in the mouth (tetras under 4 cm, rasboras, nano species)
invertebrates (shrimp and small snails will be eaten)
very small or slow-moving bottom dwellers competing for territory
conspecifics in tanks under 400 L (territorial aggression escalates)
overly aggressive cichlids that will harass a nocturnal catfish
delicate long-finned fish (Synodontis spines can injure them)

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species photo of S. decorus; shows adult specimen with characteristic elongated dorsal fin filament and spotted juvenile-to-adult transitional patterning.
Wikimedia Commons image of S. decorus in aquarium; verify attribution on the commons page before use.

summary: The Clown Squeaker is a large, eye-catching catfish from the Congo Basin, recognisable by the long trailing filament on its dorsal fin and a bold spotted pattern in juveniles that fades as the fish matures. Unlike most members of its genus, adults tend to become more active during the day. Produces audible squeaking sounds when startled.
careNotes:
The Clown Squeaker (Synodontis decorus) is one of the more visually distinctive members of the Mochokidae family, recognised by the exceptionally long trailing filament on the dorsal fin that develops as the fish matures. Juveniles display a bold high-contrast pattern of black spots and blotches on a pale cream or grey background; this pattern gradually fades as the fish ages, with adults developing a plainer brownish-grey coloration. Because juveniles and adults look so different, they are sometimes sold under different or confused names, so it is worth knowing both life stages. Care should be taken not to confuse this species with Synodontis angelicus, which retains white spots on a dark body throughout its life, or with the much smaller Synodontis nigriventris, the obligate upside-down catfish.

An adult size of 25–30 cm total length means the Clown Squeaker requires a tank of at least 300 L with a footprint of 150 cm or more; many experienced keepers recommend 400–500 L for long-term success. Water quality must be excellent: robust filtration achieving high turnover (8–10× the tank volume per hour) is advisable, reflecting the well-oxygenated currents of the Congo Basin. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are recommended. A temperature of 23–28°C with soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5) suits this species well.

Like all Synodontis, S. decorus produces audible clicking or squeaking sounds by rubbing the base of the pectoral spine against the pectoral girdle — this is normal behaviour, typically heard during handling or when the fish is startled. Unlike most members of the genus, which tend to remain strictly nocturnal throughout their lives, mature S. decorus progressively adopts more active daytime habits and can be observed foraging during daylight hours, making it an unusually displayable fish for its size.

The species is generally solitary and territorial toward conspecifics. If multiple individuals are kept together, each fish should have its own cave or shelter, and the tank must be large enough to allow established territories that prevent escalating aggression. Copper-based medications should be avoided or used with great care, as scaleless and semi-scaleless catfish are typically more sensitive to copper than scaled species. Juveniles can be fin-nippy with long-finned tankmates.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Synodontis decorus is rarely achieved and is considered very difficult. In the wild, S. decorus is suspected to be a substrate spawner in river habitats, but detailed spawning observations are not well-documented. Some Synodontis species are cuckoo spawners (parasitising mouthbrooding cichlid spawns), but this has not been confirmed as the primary strategy for S. decorus in the available literature. Hormonal induction (HCG injection) has reportedly been used to induce spawning in West African catfish species at specialist facilities, but no reliable home-aquarium protocol exists for this species. Not recommended as a breeding project for typical aquarists.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis decorus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 30 cm), water parameters (22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm/2–12 dH), native range (Congo Basin), habitat, diet, behaviour (juvenile nocturnal to adult diurnal transition), and compatibility notes. Taxonomy confirmed as Mochokidae, Siluriformes.

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis decorus
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (30 cm TL), native range (Congo Basin, DRC, Cameroon), taxonomy (Mochokidae, Siluriformes), temperature range (22–28°C), and diet classification (omnivore). Confirmed IUCN status as Least Concern.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Synodontis Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Synodontis genus care overview; confirmed stridulation behaviour ('squeaker' common name derivation), general husbandry recommendations for large Synodontis species, diet preferences, and compatibility with Congo Basin community tanks.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Synodontis decorus profile
additional.notes: Species profile confirming adult size range 25–30 cm, juvenile colour pattern (bold black spots on pale background fading at maturity), distinction from S. angelicus and S. nigriventris, and elongated dorsal fin filament as a diagnostic feature. Tank size recommendation of 300 L minimum confirmed.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Synodontis genus overview
additional.notes: Supplementary genus-level reference for Mochokidae stridulation mechanism (pectoral spine base rubbing against cleithrum/coracoid of pectoral girdle producing audible sound), diurnal behaviour shift noted as atypical among Synodontis, and general notes on cuckoo-spawning vs substrate-spawning strategies across the genus.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Clown Squeaker is essentially unreported under normal aquarium conditions. In the wild, the species is suspected to be a substrate spawner, though detailed observations of its spawning behaviour are not well documented. Cuckoo spawning — a strategy used by some Synodontis species, in which eggs are mixed into the spawns of mouthbrooding cichlids and the catfish fry are raised by the host — has not been confirmed as a reproductive strategy for S. decorus. Hormonal induction has reportedly been attempted at specialist facilities, but no reliable home-aquarium breeding protocol exists for this species.
